title: Two Sum
statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. Assume each input has exactly one solution and you may not use the same element twice.
hint: Walk the array once. For each value, ask: have I already seen `target - value`? A hash map of value -> index makes that lookup O(1).
pythonTests:
r = two_sum([2, 7, 11, 15], 9)
assert sorted(r) == [0, 1], f'Expected [0, 1], got {r}'
r = two_sum([3, 2, 4], 6)
assert sorted(r) == [1, 2], f'Expected [1, 2], got {r}'
r = two_sum([3, 3], 6)
assert sorted(r) == [0, 1], f'Expected [0, 1], got {r}'
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')

title: Reverse String
statement: Reverse the input string in place. The input is given as a list of characters.
constraints: s[i] is a printable ASCII character.
hint: Use two pointers: one at the start, one at the end. Swap, move both, repeat until they meet.

starters.Python:
def reverse_string(s):
    # TODO: reverse s in place.
    pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    s = list('hello')
    reverse_string(s)
    print(s)

pythonTests:
s = list('hello')
reverse_string(s)
assert s == ['o','l','l','e','h'], f'Got {s}'
s = list('A')
reverse_string(s)
assert s == ['A'], f'Got {s}'
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')

title: Valid Palindrome
statement: A phrase is a palindrome if, after lowercasing and removing all non-alphanumeric characters, it reads the same forward and backward. Return true if the input string is a palindrome, false otherwise.

examples.input: s = 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama'

hint: Two pointers moving toward each other. Skip non-alphanumeric, compare lowercased characters, stop on mismatch.
pythonTests:
assert is_palindrome('A man, a plan, a canal: Panama') is True
assert is_palindrome('race a car') is False
assert is_palindrome(' ') is True
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')

title: FizzBuzz
statement: Return a list of strings of length n where for each i from 1 to n: 'FizzBuzz' if i is divisible by 15, 'Fizz' if divisible by 3, 'Buzz' if divisible by 5, otherwise the number itself as a string.
hint: Check divisibility by 15 first to avoid double-checks. A single loop, no DP needed.
pythonTests:
assert fizz_buzz(5) == ['1','2','Fizz','4','Buzz']
r = fizz_buzz(15)
assert r[-1] == 'FizzBuzz' and r[2] == 'Fizz' and r[4] == 'Buzz', f'Got {r}'
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')

title: Valid Parentheses
statement: Given a string containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the string is valid. A string is valid if open brackets are closed by the same type, and in the correct order.
hint: Push openers onto a stack. On a closer, the top must match. End with an empty stack.
pythonTests:
assert is_valid('()[]{}') is True
assert is_valid('(]') is False
assert is_valid('([{}])') is True
assert is_valid('(') is False
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')

title: Binary Search
statement: Given a sorted array nums and a target value, return the index of target. If it doesn't exist, return -1. Solve in O(log n) time.
constraints: nums is sorted in strictly increasing order.
hint: Maintain low/high pointers. Compute mid = low + (high - low) / 2 to avoid overflow. Move the bound that doesn't contain target.
pythonTests:
assert search([-1, 0, 3, 5, 9, 12], 9) == 4
assert search([-1, 0, 3, 5, 9, 12], 2) == -1
assert search([5], 5) == 0
assert search([5], -5) == -1
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')

title: Climbing Stairs
statement: You are climbing a staircase of n steps. Each time you can climb 1 or 2 steps. Return the number of distinct ways to reach the top.
hint: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2). It is Fibonacci with different base cases. Use two variables, not an array.
pythonTests:
assert climb_stairs(1) == 1
assert climb_stairs(2) == 2
assert climb_stairs(4) == 5
assert climb_stairs(10) == 89
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
statement: You have prices[i] = the price of a stock on day i. You may buy on one day and sell on a later day. Return the maximum profit. If no profit is possible, return 0.

examples.explanation: Buy on day 2 at 1, sell on day 5 at 6.

hint: Track the minimum price seen so far. For each day, the best sell-today profit is price - minSoFar.
pythonTests:
assert max_profit([7, 1, 5, 3, 6, 4]) == 5
assert max_profit([7, 6, 4, 3, 1]) == 0
assert max_profit([1]) == 0
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')

title: Maximum Subarray
statement: Given an integer array nums, return the largest sum of any contiguous non-empty subarray.
hint: Kadane: at each index, either extend the running sum or reset to the current value if extending makes things worse. Track the best so far.
pythonTests:
assert max_subarray([-2, 1, -3, 4, -1, 2, 1, -5, 4]) == 6
assert max_subarray([1]) == 1
assert max_subarray([5, 4, -1, 7, 8]) == 23
assert max_subarray([-1, -2, -3]) == -1
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')

title: Merge Two Sorted Arrays
statement: Given two sorted integer arrays a and b, return a single sorted array that contains all elements of both. Aim for O(n + m).
constraints: a and b are each sorted in non-decreasing order.
hint: Two pointers, one per array. Append the smaller current element to the result, advance that pointer. Drain the rest at the end.
pythonTests:
assert merge_sorted([1, 3, 5], [2, 4, 6]) == [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
assert merge_sorted([], [1]) == [1]
assert merge_sorted([2, 2], [1, 1]) == [1, 1, 2, 2]
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')

title: Group Anagrams
statement: Given a list of strings, group the anagrams together. Return the groups in any order.
hint: Two strings are anagrams iff their sorted characters match. Use that sorted string as a key in a hash map of lists.
pythonTests:
r = group_anagrams(['eat','tea','tan','ate','nat','bat'])
norm = sorted(tuple(sorted(g)) for g in r)
assert norm == [('ate','eat','tea'), ('bat',), ('nat','tan')], f'Got {norm}'
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
statement: Given a string s, return the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.
hint: Sliding window: keep a set of seen characters and two pointers. When a duplicate enters, advance the left pointer past the previous occurrence.
pythonTests:
assert length_of_longest_substring('abcabcbb') == 3
assert length_of_longest_substring('bbbbb') == 1
assert length_of_longest_substring('pwwkew') == 3
assert length_of_longest_substring('') == 0
print('__CODE_BUDDY_ALL_PASSED__')
